content:
# Genesis Chapter 34 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 19v–20r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_034_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_034_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 31–32 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0035

---

**1** Now Dinah the daughter of Leah, whom she had borne to Jacob, went out to see the daughters of the land.

**2** And Shechem the son of Hamor the Hivite, the prince of the land, saw her, and he took her and lay with her and violated her.

**3** And his soul clung to Dinah the daughter of Jacob, and he loved the young woman and spoke tenderly to the young woman.

**4** So Shechem spoke to his father Hamor, saying, "Get me this young woman as a wife."

**5** Now Jacob heard that he had defiled his daughter Dinah. But his sons were with his livestock in the field, so Jacob held his peace until they came.

**6** And Hamor the father of Shechem went out to Jacob to speak with him.

**7** The sons of Jacob came in from the field when they heard it, and the men were indignant and very angry, because he had committed an outrage in Israel by lying with Jacob's daughter — a thing that ought not to be done.

**8** But Hamor spoke with them, saying, "The soul of my son Shechem longs for your daughter. Please give her to him as a wife.

**9** Make marriages with us. Give your daughters to us, and take our daughters for yourselves.

**10** You shall dwell with us, and the land shall be open to you. Dwell and move about in it and get property in it."

**11** Shechem also said to her father and to her brothers, "Let me find favor in your eyes, and whatever you say to me I will give.

**12** Ask of me as great a bride-price and gift as you will, and I will give whatever you say to me. Only give me the young woman as a wife."

**13** The sons of Jacob answered Shechem and his father Hamor with deceit, because he had defiled their sister Dinah.

**14** They said to them, "We cannot do this thing, to give our sister to one who is uncircumcised, for that would be a disgrace to us.

**15** Only on this condition will we consent to you: if you will become as we are, by every male among you being circumcised.

**16** Then we will give our daughters to you, and we will take your daughters to ourselves, and we will dwell with you and become one people.

**17** But if you will not listen to us and be circumcised, then we will take our daughter and go."

**18** Their words pleased Hamor and Hamor's son Shechem.

**19** And the young man did not delay to do the thing, because he delighted in Jacob's daughter. He was the most honored of all his father's house.

**20** So Hamor and his son Shechem came to the gate of their city and spoke to the men of their city, saying,

**21** "These men are at peace with us. Let them dwell in the land and move about in it, for behold, the land is large enough for them. Let us take their daughters as wives, and let us give them our daughters.

**22** Only on this condition will the men consent to dwell with us to become one people: that every male among us be circumcised as they are circumcised.

**23** Will not their livestock, their property, and all their animals be ours? Only let us consent to them, and they will dwell with us."

**24** And all who went out of the gate of his city listened to Hamor and his son Shechem, and every male was circumcised, all who went out of the gate of his city.

**25** On the third day, when they were in pain, two of the sons of Jacob, Simeon and Levi, Dinah's brothers, each took his sword and came against the city while it felt secure and killed all the males.

**26** They killed Hamor and his son Shechem with the edge of the sword and took Dinah out of Shechem's house and went away.

**27** The sons of Jacob came upon the slain and plundered the city, because they had defiled their sister.

**28** They took their flocks and their herds and their donkeys, and whatever was in the city and in the field.

**29** All their wealth, all their little ones and their wives, all that was in the houses, they captured and plundered.

**30** Then Jacob said to Simeon and Levi, "You have brought trouble on me by making me stink to the inhabitants of the land, to the Canaanites and the Perizzites. My numbers are few, and if they gather themselves against me and attack me, I shall be destroyed, both I and my household."

**31** But they said, "Should he treat our sister like a prostitute?"

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Source Manuscripts

This translation was produced from direct reading of four manuscript image sources:

1. **Leningrad Codex (Codex Leningradensis, B19A)**, dated c. 1008 CE. West Semitic Research Project photographs by Bruce E. Zuckerman. Genesis 34 spans Folios 19v–20r.

   **Folio 19v** (GEN_034_page_001.jpg): The verso of Folio 19, which carries the conclusion of Genesis 33 and then the opening of Genesis 34. The three-column layout with Tiberian vocalization (pointing) and cantillation marks (te'amim) is clearly legible throughout. Genesis 34 begins with Dinah's departure to see the daughters of the land (v. 1) and continues through the narrative of Shechem's assault (v. 2), the language of his attachment and love for Dinah (vv. 3–4), Jacob's hearing and silence (v. 5), Hamor's approach (v. 6), the sons' anger and the description of nevalah be-Yisra'el (v. 7), Hamor's proposal for intermarriage (vv. 8–10), Shechem's offer of any bride-price (vv. 11–12), and the sons' deceitful counter-demand for circumcision (vv. 13–17). The page carries through to approximately verse 20. Masorah parva annotations are visible in the inter-column margins. The chapter break from Genesis 33 is marked with the standard paragraph marker (פ or ס).

   **Folio 20r** (GEN_034_page_002.jpg): The recto of Folio 20. This page carries the completion of Genesis 34, from verse 21 onward: Hamor and Shechem's address to the men of the city at the gate (vv. 20–24), the account of the massacre by Simeon and Levi on the third day (v. 25), the killing of Hamor and Shechem and the rescue of Dinah (v. 26), the plundering of the city by Jacob's sons (vv. 27–29), Jacob's rebuke to Simeon and Levi (v. 30), and the sons' defiant final question (v. 31). The page then continues into the opening verses of Genesis 35. The text of this closing section is clearly legible; the three-column structure is maintained and the Masoretic pointing is consistent throughout.

2. **Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209)** — Septuagint/LXX, 4th century CE. Two pages were read directly.

   **Page 31** (GEN_034_vaticanus_page_001.jpg): Vaticanus page 31 (digi.vatlib.it page pa_0035). The chapter heading marker "CAP. XXXIIII" is visible at the right margin. This page carries the LXX text corresponding to the conclusion of Genesis 33 and the opening of Genesis 34, running through approximately verse 17. The continuous uncial Greek text in three columns is legible. The LXX presents the narrative substantially consistent with the MT, though with several notable lexical choices detailed below.

   **Page 32** (GEN_034_vaticanus_page_002.jpg): Vaticanus page 32 (digi.vatlib.it page pa_0036). The page heading "Γεν ΧΧΧΙV" (or equivalent) is visible. This page carries the remainder of Genesis 34 (vv. 18–31) and continues into Genesis 35. The text is legible throughout, with the closing exchange between Jacob and his sons in verses 30–31 clearly visible near the bottom of the relevant column before the chapter transitions.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

**1. The Three Verbs of Verse 2 — וַיִּקַּח אֹתָהּ וַיִּשְׁכַּב אֹתָהּ וַיְעַנֶּהָ**

The Hebrew of verse 2 presents three consecutive verbs describing what Shechem did to Dinah: וַיִּקַּח אֹתָהּ (vayyiqqach otah — "and he took her"), וַיִּשְׁכַּב אֹתָהּ (vayyishkav otah — "and he lay with her"), וַיְעַנֶּהָ (vay'anneha — "and he violated/humiliated her"). The progression is deliberate and escalating. The third verb, עִנָּה (innah), is from the root עָנָה (anah II — "to oppress, to afflict, to humble, to violate") and is the standard biblical term for sexual violation by force (compare its use in 2 Samuel 13:12 in the parallel story of Tamar's rape by Amnon, and Deuteronomy 22:24, 29). The formal translation renders: "he took her and lay with her and violated her" — preserving all three verbs in sequence without collapsing them or smoothing the accumulation of violence. The LXX renders καὶ ἔλαβεν αὐτήν καὶ ἐκοιμήθη μετ᾽ αὐτῆς καὶ ἐταπείνωσεν αὐτήν (kai elaben autēn kai ekoimēthē met' autēs kai etapeinōsen autēn — "and he took her and lay with her and humiliated/degraded her"), consistent with the MT's three-verb structure. The LXX's choice of ταπεινόω (tapeinoō — "to humble, to degrade, to humiliate") rather than a term specifically denoting sexual violence follows the MT's root precisely; the formal translation's "violated" makes the sexual violence explicit in English while remaining faithful to the semantic range of the Hebrew.

**2. "His soul clung to Dinah" and "he spoke tenderly" — וַתִּדְבַּק נַפְשׁוֹ and וַיְדַבֵּר עַל־לֵב (v. 3)**

The juxtaposition of verse 2 and verse 3 is one of the most disturbing in the Hebrew Bible. Immediately after the three verbs of violence, the narrator reports: "and his soul clung to Dinah the daughter of Jacob, and he loved the young woman and spoke tenderly to the young woman." The Hebrew phrase וַתִּדְבַּק נַפְשׁוֹ בְּדִינָה (vattidbaq nafsho be-Dinah — "and his soul clung to Dinah") uses the verb דָּבַק (davaq — "to cling, to cleave") — the same verb used of a husband cleaving to his wife in Genesis 2:24 ("and he shall cling to his wife"). The phrase וַיְדַבֵּר עַל־לֵב הַנַּעֲרָ (vayedabber 'al-lev ha-na'arah — literally "and he spoke upon the heart of the young woman," idiomatically "he spoke to the heart of the young woman" or "he spoke tenderly to her") is the same idiom used of comfort and courtship in Ruth 2:13 and Hosea 2:14. The narrator uses love-language directly after assault-language, and the reader must hold both simultaneously. The formal translation renders "his soul clung to Dinah" — preserving the davaq verb — and "spoke tenderly to the young woman" — following the idiom — without smoothing or explaining away the moral dissonance. The LXX renders ἐκολλήθη ἡ ψυχὴ αὐτοῦ (ekollēthē hē psychē autou — "his soul was joined/glued to her") and ἐλάλησεν κατὰ τὴν διάνοιαν τῆς νεάνιδος (elalēsen kata tēn dianoian tēs neanidos — "he spoke according to the mind/heart of the young woman"), consistent with the MT.

**3. "An outrage in Israel" — נְבָלָה בְּיִשְׂרָאֵל (v. 7)**

The phrase נְבָלָה בְּיִשְׂרָאֵל (nevalah be-Yisra'el — "an outrage in Israel" / "a disgraceful thing in Israel") is a technical judicial formula in the Hebrew Bible. It appears here for the first time in the canon, and subsequently in Deuteronomy 22:21 (for a woman found not to be a virgin at marriage), Joshua 7:15 (for Achan's theft), Judges 20:6, 10 (for the gang rape at Gibeah), 2 Samuel 13:12 (Tamar to Amnon: "such a thing is not done in Israel"), and Jeremiah 29:23. It functions as a quasi-legal declaration that the act in question constitutes a fundamental violation of the community's moral and social order. The remarkable feature of its first use here is the anachronism: Jacob is not yet "Israel" in the national sense, and the nation of Israel does not yet exist. The formula belongs to a later period of Israelite legal and social consciousness but is retrojected into the patriarchal narrative. The formal translation renders "an outrage in Israel" following the standard English equivalent for this formula. The LXX renders ἀνόμημα ἐν Ισραηλ (anomēma en Israēl — "a lawless deed/transgression in Israel"), consistent with the MT.

**4. "With deceit" — בְּמִרְמָה (v. 13)**

The narrator's use of בְּמִרְמָה (bemirmah — "with deceit / with cunning / treacherously") in verse 13 is a deliberate authorial intrusion. The word מִרְמָה (mirmah — "deceit, treachery, guile") is the same root used of Jacob's own deception of his father (Genesis 27:35: "your brother came with deceit [bemirmah] and took your blessing"). The narrator does not conceal the sons' deception from the reader; the word bemirmah is stated plainly before the dialogue that follows, making it clear that the demand for circumcision was a trap from the beginning. This is a morally complex narrative choice: the sons use deception to avenge violation, the narrator names the deception as such, and yet no divine judgment is pronounced on the sons for their method. Jacob's rebuke (v. 30) is pragmatic, not moral. The formal translation renders "with deceit," maintaining the word that labels what follows as fraudulent. The LXX renders μετὰ δόλου (meta dolou — "with guile/treachery"), consistent with the MT.

**5. "Uncircumcised" as an insult — עָרֵל (v. 14)**

The term עָרֵל (arel — "uncircumcised") appears in verse 14 and functions as more than a physical description: "We cannot do this thing, to give our sister to one who is uncircumcised, for that would be a disgrace to us." The word עָרֵל carries social and religious stigma throughout the Hebrew Bible (compare Judges 14:3; 1 Samuel 14:6, 17:26, 36 — where "uncircumcised Philistine" is a term of contempt). The formal translation renders "one who is uncircumcised" — preserving the term directly. The LXX renders ἀπεριτμήτῳ (aperitmētō — "uncircumcised"), consistent with the MT.

**6. The Gate Assembly (vv. 20–24)**

The setting of Hamor and Shechem's address "to the gate of their city" (v. 20) reflects the well-attested ancient Near Eastern civic institution of the city gate as the primary locus of public assembly, legal proceedings, and political decision-making (compare Ruth 4:1–12; Proverbs 31:23; Amos 5:10, 15). The assembly of "all who went out of the gate of his city" is a phrase describing the adult male citizenry — those who participated in public life. The formal translation preserves "the gate of their city" and "all who went out of the gate of his city" without modernizing the civic setting. The LXX renders ἐπὶ τὴν πύλην τῆς πόλεως αὐτῶν (epi tēn pylēn tēs poleōs autōn — "to the gate of their city"), consistent with the MT.

**7. "When they were in pain" — בִּהְיוֹתָם כֹּאֲבִים (v. 25)**

The phrase בִּהְיוֹתָם כֹּאֲבִים (biheyotam ko'avim — "when they were in pain / while they were aching") is the narrator's precise clinical note about the vulnerability created by mass adult circumcision: the men of the city were incapacitated by post-operative pain on the third day, the day of maximum inflammation. Simeon and Levi exploit this deliberately induced physical vulnerability. The formal translation renders "when they were in pain" — preserving the Hebrew's straightforward physical description and the moral weight of that exploitation. The LXX renders ἐν τῇ ἡμέρᾳ τῇ τρίτῃ ὅτε ἦσαν ἐν τῷ πόνῳ (en tē hēmera tē tritē hote ēsan en tō ponō — "on the third day when they were in pain/anguish"), consistent with the MT.

**8. "Brought trouble on me" — עֲכַרְתֶּם אֹתִי (v. 30)**

Jacob's rebuke in verse 30 uses the verb עָכַר (akar — "to trouble, to bring calamity upon, to make turbid/muddy"). The verb is rare and charged: it is the same root used of Achan in the "Valley of Achor" (Joshua 7:25–26) and of Jephthah's daughter (Judges 11:35). In context, Jacob's complaint is explicitly pragmatic — he fears that the surrounding Canaanite and Perizzite populations will now combine against his small household and destroy them. He does not say that the massacre was wrong, nor does he express grief for Dinah or for the dead. His complaint is: "you have made me odious / you have endangered me." The formal translation renders "you have brought trouble on me by making me stink to the inhabitants of the land" — preserving the verb's calamity-sense and the vivid "stink" idiom (בְּאַשְׁנִי, be'ashni — literally "to make me smell bad / to make me stench-like before") which denotes making oneself odious in the eyes of others. The LXX renders μεμίσητέ με (memisēte me — "you have made me hated"), capturing the social consequence but softening the olfactory vividness of the Hebrew idiom.

**9. The Final Question — הַכְזוֹנָה יַעֲשֶׂה אֶת־אֲחוֹתֵנוּ (v. 31)**

The chapter ends without resolution, narrative closure, or divine comment. The final word belongs to Simeon and Levi: הַכְזוֹנָה יַעֲשֶׂה אֶת־אֲחוֹתֵנוּ (hakhezonah ya'aseh et-achotenu — "Should he treat our sister like a prostitute?"). The rhetorical question is introduced by the interrogative particle הֲ (ha-) expecting a negative answer: No, he should not. The word זוֹנָה (zonah — "prostitute, harlot") is used here not as a statement about Dinah's character but as a description of how Shechem treated her — as a woman available for taking without contract, covenant, or consent. The question is fierce and unanswered; the narrator leaves Jacob and his sons in irresolvable moral tension. The formal translation renders "Should he treat our sister like a prostitute?" — preserving the interrogative force and the word "prostitute" directly. The LXX renders ὡσεὶ πόρνην χρήσονται τῇ ἀδελφῇ ἡμῶν (hōsei pornēn chrēsontai tē adelphē hēmōn — "shall they use our sister like a prostitute?"), where the LXX interestingly pluralizes ("they" rather than "he"), possibly generalizing the offense. The MT's "he" (singular) specifically indicts Shechem; the formal translation follows the MT's singular.

---

### LXX (Vaticanus) Variants — Genesis 34

Pages 31–32 of Codex Vaticanus were read directly. The continuous uncial Greek text was legible throughout. Key variants observed:

| Verse (MT) | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Translation Decision | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 34:2 | חִוִּי ("Hivite") | Χορραῖος ("Horite") | "Hivite" | LXX reads Hamor as a Horite rather than a Hivite; the MT consistently identifies Hamor as Hivite (cf. Josh 9:7, 11:3); formal translation follows MT |
| 34:3 | וַתִּדְבַּק נַפְשׁוֹ ("his soul clung") | ἐκολλήθη ἡ ψυχή ("his soul was joined/glued") | "his soul clung" | Both preserve the cleaving/joining image; formal translation follows MT's davaq with its Genesis 2:24 resonance |
| 34:7 | נְבָלָה בְּיִשְׂרָאֵל ("an outrage in Israel") | ἀνόμημα ἐν Ισραηλ ("a lawless deed in Israel") | "an outrage in Israel" | LXX legalizes the term ("lawless deed"); MT nevalah is the older, broader formula of moral disgrace |
| 34:13 | בְּמִרְמָה ("with deceit") | μετὰ δόλου ("with guile") | "with deceit" | Consistent rendering; both Greek dolou and Hebrew mirmah denote deliberate deception |
| 34:25 | שִׁמְעוֹן וְלֵוִי אַחֵי דִינָה ("Simeon and Levi, brothers of Dinah") | Συμεων καὶ Λευι οἱ ἀδελφοὶ Δεινας ("Simeon and Levi, the brothers of Dinah") | "Simeon and Levi, Dinah's brothers" | Consistent; LXX adds the definite article before "brothers," emphasizing their identity as Dinah's brothers specifically |
| 34:30 | בְּאַשְׁנִי ("making me stink/odious") | μεμίσητέ με ("you have made me hated") | "making me stink" | LXX softens the olfactory idiom to a general social concept; MT preserves the vividness of the image; formal translation retains MT's phrasing |
| 34:31 | הַכְזוֹנָה יַעֲשֶׂה ("should he treat") — singular | ὡσεὶ πόρνην χρήσονται — plural | "should he treat" (singular) | LXX pluralizes ("shall they treat"), possibly generalizing from Shechem to the Shechemites; MT's singular specifically targets Shechem; formal translation follows MT |

---

### Structural and Theological Notes

**The Narrative Architecture of Genesis 34**

Genesis 34 is constructed in four movements, each with its own moral focal point:

- **Movement 1 (vv. 1–7):** The assault. Dinah goes out; Shechem sees, takes, and violates her; his soul clings to her; Jacob is silent; the sons are incensed. The chapter opens with three characters introduced in rapid succession: Dinah (named, identified by her mother Leah and her father Jacob), Shechem (named, identified by his father Hamor and his civic status), and Jacob's sons (named collectively as those with the livestock). The structure immediately highlights what the chapter will turn on: Dinah, who goes out as an agent of her own curiosity, becomes an object of action performed on her; from verse 2 onward she is a thing done to — seized, violated, loved, spoken to, demanded, taken back, "taken out of Shechem's house" (v. 26). She speaks no word in the chapter. She is its center and its silence.

- **Movement 2 (vv. 8–17):** The negotiation. Hamor proposes intermarriage and territorial integration; Shechem adds unlimited bride-price; the brothers respond with the circumcision demand. The narrator has already told the reader that the brothers speak "with deceit" (v. 13). Their offer of integration — "we will give our daughters to you, we will take your daughters, we will dwell with you and become one people" (v. 16) — is made in bad faith. The chapter explores a profoundly uncomfortable question: what does legitimate response to illegitimate violation look like? The brothers' answer is that deception is a morally available tool against those who have violated their sister.

- **Movement 3 (vv. 18–26):** The massacre. The Shechemites comply; Simeon and Levi exploit the induced vulnerability; every male is killed; Dinah is recovered; the city is subsequently plundered. The text gives no divine sanction to the slaughter, but neither does it report divine condemnation. The narrator is silent on the moral question. Only later — in Genesis 49:5–7 — will Jacob pronounce judgment on Simeon and Levi's violence: "their anger was fierce and their wrath was cruel," and the tribes associated with them will be scattered in Israel.

- **Movement 4 (vv. 27–31):** The aftermath. The broader family plunders the city; Jacob rebukes Simeon and Levi on purely pragmatic grounds; they answer with the unanswered question about their sister's honor. The chapter ends not with resolution but with two positions — Jacob's fear and pragmatism, the brothers' fierce insistence on Dinah's dignity — suspended against each other in permanent moral tension.

**Jacob's Silence and the Question of Patriarchal Failure**

Verse 5 records Jacob's response to learning of his daughter's violation: "Jacob held his peace until they came." The Hebrew is literally וְיַעֲקֹב שָׁמַע ... וְיַעֲקֹב הֶחֱרִשׁ (ve-Ya'aqov shama ... ve-Ya'aqov hechresh — "Jacob heard ... Jacob was silent"). The verb הֶחֱרִשׁ (hechresh) means to be silent, to hold one's tongue, to refrain from speech or action. Whether this silence is prudential (waiting for his sons) or passive (failing to act) is deliberately left ambiguous by the narrator. Jacob's later rebuke (v. 30) addresses only his own security and reputation. He does not speak for Dinah, does not name the wrong done to her, does not condemn Shechem by name. The parallel silence and speech of Dinah's brothers — indignant in verse 7, agents of savage revenge in verse 25 — contrasts with Jacob's controlled inaction. The narrative assigns no single moral position as correct.

**"One People" — the Rejected Integration (vv. 16, 22)**

The offer of becoming "one people" (לְעַם אֶחָד, le-am echad) through intermarriage appears twice in the chapter — in the brothers' fraudulent offer (v. 16) and in Hamor and Shechem's address to the city (v. 22). The phrase marks one of the key theological preoccupations of the Pentateuch: the question of Israel's distinctiveness among the nations. In the broader narrative arc, Jacob's household is in the process of becoming "Israel" — the people of God in covenantal distinction from the surrounding nations. The offer of merger through marriage represents a path that the narrative consistently closes off, here through violent means. The circumcision requirement, understood in the Shechemites' ears as a social integration requirement, functions in the brothers' minds as a weapon. The covenant sign of Israel is used against outsiders who attempt to claim it without the prior covenantal relationship it signifies.

**Genesis 34 and the Tamar Parallel (2 Samuel 13)**

The story of Dinah bears unmistakable structural parallels with the story of Tamar's rape by her half-brother Amnon in 2 Samuel 13. In both narratives: (1) a woman of high family is sexually violated; (2) a male family member with political power (Jacob / David) is silent and does not act; (3) a brother acts violently to avenge the dishonor; (4) the avenger uses a deceptive stratagem; (5) the father mourns the consequences but not primarily for the victim. The formula "a thing that ought not to be done" in Genesis 34:7 reappears verbatim in Tamar's protest to Amnon in 2 Samuel 13:12: "such a thing is not done in Israel." The parallel suggests that the Dinah narrative is functioning as a paradigmatic case — the nevalah be-Yisra'el formula and its consequences — that will recur across Israelite history.

---

— LOGOS
